chunk_summary: Guidelines for managing infections in children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM), emphasizing antibiotic stewardship, reassessment timelines, and syndrome-specific treatment durations.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of early infection triggers in complicated SAM, even without fever.
Learn standardized antibiotic prescribing and reassessment protocols for SAM management.
Identify syndrome-specific antibiotic durations and criteria for narrowing therapy.
Recognize mandatory reassessment triggers for non-improvement by day 5.
key_facts: In complicated SAM, bacterial infection should be assumed even without typical signs.
Reassess antibiotic therapy at 48–72 hours and day 5 to adjust treatment based on clinical progress.
Minimum antibiotic durations: sepsis ≥7 days, UTI 7–10 days, meningitis 14–21 days, osteomyelitis ~4 weeks.
Early physiologic danger signs include hypothermia, recurrent hypoglycemia, and persistent lethargy.
Cultures should be collected before antibiotics if feasible, but therapy must not be delayed in unstable children.
reference_quote: In complicated SAM, assume bacterial infection even when typical signs are absent. Antibiotic prescribing should be standardized and time-linked to reassessment, so that trainees neither undertreat silent bacteremia nor prolong broad-spectrum therapy unnecessarily. (Source: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Operational guidelines on facility-based management of children with severe acute malnutrition, 2011)
